a:r.a:t: So every request, no matter what, follows this exact path. The file path at the bottom of the slide traces the call chain: api/main.py receives the HTTP request, app/service.py's process_turn() at line 819 handles session hydration and routing, router/router.py classifies intent, and then runtime/kernel.py sets up agent context before runtime/query_loop.py executes the actual agent logic. See that fork in the road in the middle? That is the router -- the single most important decision point in the system. It asks: is this BASIC, meaning just chat with no tools needed? Or is it AGENT, meaning this needs real processing, tools, maybe multiple steps? The reason this split matters architecturally is cost and latency. A basic request makes one LLM call and returns in roughly 200 milliseconds with zero tool overhead. An agent request enters the full ReAct loop, which might make 3 to 10 LLM calls with tool executions in between. By having the router make this decision upfront, the system avoids paying agent-mode costs for simple questions -- which in production tend to be about 40 percent of all traffic. The five files in the call chain map to the three runtime layers: service.py is RuntimeService (session and routing), kernel.py is RuntimeKernel (context setup and agent orchestration), and query_loop.py is QueryLoop (execution engine). Each layer can be tested, scaled, and modified independently. If you need to change how routing works, you touch router.py and service.py without affecting query_loop.py. If you need to add a new execution mode, you modify query_loop.py without touching routing. This separation of concerns is what makes the system maintainable at scale.
